examples.title: Simple Process Creation using System Call
examples.description: Demonstrates how system calls work in OS - creating a new process using fork()
examples.code:
SYSTEM CALL CreateProcess
BEGIN
  // User program requests new process via system call
  PRINT "Parent Process: Requesting new process..."
  
  // Kernel creates process control block (PCB)
  pcb = ALLOCATE_PROCESS_CONTROL_BLOCK()
  pcb.process_id = GENERATE_UNIQUE_ID()
  pcb.state = READY
  pcb.program_counter = ADDRESS_OF_MAIN
  
  // Allocate memory for new process
  pcb.memory_address = ALLOCATE_MEMORY(process_size)
  
  // Copy parent process to child
  COPY_MEMORY(parent_process, pcb.memory_address)
  
  // Add to ready queue
  ADD_TO_READY_QUEUE(pcb)
  
  RETURN pcb.process_id
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the system call mechanism where a user program requests the OS kernel to create a new process. The kernel allocates resources (PCB, memory), initializes the process control block, and adds it to the ready queue for CPU scheduling.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is process memory size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is size of PCB + process memory

examples.title: Memory Allocation Simulation
examples.description: Simple simulation of how OS allocates memory to processes
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates basic memory management functions of an OS. It demonstrates allocation (finding space for processes), tracking allocated memory blocks, and deallocation (freeing memory when process completes). The 'first-fit' allocation strategy is shown here.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of processes

examples.title: CPU Scheduling Simulation (Round Robin)
examples.description: Demonstrates time-sharing concept where CPU time is divided into quantum slices
examples.explanation: This C program simulates Round Robin CPU scheduling, a key algorithm in time-sharing operating systems. Each process gets equal time slice (quantum) on the CPU. This ensures no process monopolizes the CPU and provides fair time-sharing among multiple processes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n * q) where n is number of processes and q is time quantum

examples.title: Device Driver Interface
examples.description: Shows how OS manages I/O devices through device drivers
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates how operating systems manage I/O devices through device drivers. The driver acts as an intermediary between user applications and hardware devices, handling commands, managing buffers, and processing interrupts to complete I/O operations efficiently.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for command preparation, variable for I/O completion
